items.q: After pre-mRNA splicing in eukaryotes, which sequences remain in the mature mRNA?
items.misconception: Introns remain because they encode the protein; exons are removed as junk.
items.random_wrong: Both introns and exons are removed and the cell uses just the cap and tail.
items.why: Students confuse the EX- (expressed) and IN- prefixes; the naming is the opposite of what intuition suggests.

items.q: What is the purpose of the refractory period after an action potential?
items.correct: It ensures action potentials travel in one direction only and limits the maximum firing frequency.
items.misconception: The refractory period exists because the cell runs out of energy and needs a rest.
items.random_wrong: The refractory period blocks all signals from reaching the neurone.
items.why: Students think of it as a 'rest break' rather than a functional feature ensuring unidirectional propagation.

items.q: Why are food chains usually limited to about 4 or 5 trophic levels?
items.correct: Only ~10 percent of energy is transferred between trophic levels; eventually too little energy remains to support another level.
items.misconception: There aren't enough types of animals to fill more trophic levels.
items.random_wrong: Larger animals always eat the next ones up, and there aren't enough giant animals.
items.why: Students don't grasp the energy-loss aspect of food chains; they think the limit is biological diversity rather than thermodynamic efficiency.

items.q: Does insulin raise blood glucose to give the body energy?
items.correct: No — insulin lowers blood glucose by helping cells take it up and store it as glycogen.
items.random_wrong: Insulin destroys glucose so it cannot be used.
items.why: Students assume a hormone linked to glucose must increase it, but insulin reduces blood glucose; glucagon is the hormone that raises it.

items.q: Does the body control blood glucose using positive feedback?
items.correct: No — blood glucose is kept steady by negative feedback, where a rise triggers a response that lowers it (and vice versa).
items.misconception: Blood glucose is controlled by positive feedback that amplifies changes.
items.random_wrong: Blood glucose is controlled only by the nervous system, not hormones.
items.why: Students mix up the feedback types; homeostasis uses negative feedback to reverse changes and restore the set point.

items.q: Adrenaline binds to receptors on the membrane and activates:
items.why: Correct answer: Adenylyl cyclase to make cAMP. “DNA directly” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Which gland is referred to as the master gland?
items.why: Correct answer: Pituitary. “Pancreas” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.
